bias_check.assessment: The core thesis claims 'sufficient mechanical redundancy to maintain positive system pressure even during periods of peak demand or localized equipment maintenance.' The redundancy claim is currently not operative — this is a direct measurement, not an interpretation. Three pumps at 99% load with zero headroom means any single additional failure immediately breaches Kill Switch #1. While the system IS currently maintaining pressure and quality (the thesis has not been falsified), the qualifier 'even during localized equipment maintenance' is actively contradicted by the zero-margin state during Pump 4 maintenance. The thesis is WEAKENING, not CONTESTED, because the evidence is directionally consistent — all negative signals point the same way, and the positive signals (pressure adequate, quality good) are lagging indicators that confirm current function but not future resilience.

Three pumps at 99% load with zero headroom means any single additional failure immediately breaches Kill Switch #1. While the system IS currently maintaining pressure and quality (the thesis has not been falsified), the qualifier 'even during localized equipment maintenance' is actively contradicted by the zero-margin state during Pump 4 maintenance. The thesis is WEAKENING, not CONTESTED, because the evidence is directionally consistent — all negative signals point the same way, and the positive signals (pressure adequate, quality good) are lagging indicators that confirm current function but not future resilience.
_layer4_raw.action_reasoning: The system is operating at critical stress with zero redundancy, one pump showing pre-existing vibration vulnerability at extreme loading, and an operator actively minimizing the severity of the situation. The action window is hours, not days — any single additional adverse event triggers cascading failure. REDUCE_EXPOSURE means: (1) immediately acquire Pump 4 restoration timeline and baseline pressure data, (2) prepare contingency plans for demand reduction and emergency pump capacity, (3) if Pump 4 restoration timeline exceeds 24 hours or Pump 3 vibration exceeds 25 Hz on next reading, escalate to EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN-equivalent actions (demand curtailment, mutual aid activation). This is consistent with WEAKENING thesis status — the situation demands active risk reduction, not passive monitoring.

unresolved_tensions.description: Kill Switch #5 (Divergent Inventory Paradox) is unresolvable without baseline system pressure. Reservoir at 94.1% meets condition 1. Condition 2 is unknown. If baseline was >82.75 PSI, this kill switch may already be triggered — which would change thesis status from WEAKENING to potentially FALSIFIED.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Acquisition of baseline system pressure data. If baseline >82.75 PSI, Kill Switch #5 is triggered and thesis status escalates immediately.

unresolved_tensions.description: Pump 4 restoration timeline is unknown. The difference between 'returns in 6 hours' and 'returns in 3 days' is the difference between a survivable stress window and a probable cascading failure.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Any communication from MWA about Pump 4 status. If no ETR is published within 12 hours, the planned-maintenance null hypothesis weakens significantly.

unresolved_tensions.description: Pump 3 vibration trajectory is unknown. 22.4 Hz could be stable or rapidly approaching the 25 Hz warning band. Without time-series data, we cannot assess imminence of Kill Switch #2.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Next Pump 3 vibration reading. If >24 Hz, Kill Switch #2 escalates to imminent. If stable at ~22 Hz, the pump-specific vulnerability is being managed by the valve adjustment.

unresolved_tensions.description: Multi-sector hydrant pressure data is absent. Kill Switch #3 cannot be assessed beyond Sector B. Elevated or distant sectors may already be experiencing pressure degradation invisible to current monitoring.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Any hydrant flow test data or pressure complaint from sectors other than B. Any Fire Dispatch operational adjustment (tanker staging, water hauling) would be a high-fidelity negative indicator.

monitoring_triggers: Pump 3 vibration exceeds 25 Hz → escalate to EMERGENCY, Kill Switch #2 approaching
Any pump trips (load on remaining units exceeds 115%) → Kill Switch #1 TRIGGERED, escalate to EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN
System pressure drops below 55 PSI → accelerated approach to Kill Switch #4, escalate monitoring to 15-minute intervals
Baseline pressure obtained and shows >20% decline from current 66.2 PSI (i.e., baseline was >82.75 PSI) → Kill Switch #5 TRIGGERED
MWA issues conservation notice or boil water advisory → confirms situation worse than public statement indicated, escalate
Pump 4 not restored within 24 hours from now → planned-maintenance null hypothesis weakens, elevate bear pressure to 80+
Any sector hydrant pressure below 30 PSI → approaching Kill Switch #3, escalate
Fire Dispatch stages water tankers or activates mutual aid → independent confirmation of system failure, Kill Switch #3 likely triggered
Ambient temperature exceeds 95°F → demand pressure amplification on zero-margin system, escalate
_layer4_raw.biggest_uncertainty: The baseline system pressure. This single missing data point determines whether Kill Switch #5 is already triggered (thesis falsified) or not approaching (thesis weakening but viable). Every other assessment is downstream of this unknown.
_layer4_raw.what_would_change_assessment: Three things would materially change the assessment: (1) Baseline pressure revealed as <78 PSI → Kill Switch #5 not approaching, bear pressure drops to ~65, thesis status remains WEAKENING but falsification probability drops to ~15%. (2) Pump 4 returns to service within 12 hours → system returns to four-pump operation, bear pressure drops to ~40, thesis status upgrades to STABLE (with permanent modification noting zero-margin vulnerability during maintenance). (3) Pump 3 vibration exceeds 25 Hz on next reading → escalate to EMERGENCY, bear pressure jumps to 85+, thesis status moves to CONTESTED or FALSIFIED depending on rate of deterioration.
_layer4_raw.final_report: The Regional Water Distribution Network is in a zero-redundancy state with all three active pumps at 99% capacity, one discrete event away from cascading mechanical failure that would immediately breach Kill Switch #1. Pump 3 shows a pump-specific vibration vulnerability (22.4 Hz, nearly double Pumps 1-2 at similar loads) that the valve adjustment may not resolve, and MWA is actively misrepresenting the severity of the situation as 'routine maintenance.' The call is REDUCE_EXPOSURE with an action window of hours: acquire Pump 4 restoration timeline and baseline system pressure immediately, because baseline pressure alone could reveal Kill Switch #5 is already triggered. The central paradox is that the system is simultaneously proving it can deliver service under extreme stress AND proving it does so with zero margin — which means the thesis of 'sufficient redundancy during maintenance' is not currently supported by operational reality, regardless of outcome.

player_analysis.stated_position: Routine maintenance underway. No service interruptions.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Three pumps running at 98.5-99.1% load with zero redundancy. Maintenance crew actively adjusting flow control valves on Pump 3 to manage vibration anomaly at 22.4 Hz. Pump 4 offline with no published restoration timeline. No emergency procurement or emergency staffing signals visible in available data.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: SEVERE. 'Routine maintenance' is irreconcilable with 99% loading across all active pumps and zero redundancy. The word 'routine' implies planned, controlled conditions with adequate margin. The operational data shows a system at its absolute ceiling with a developing vibration anomaly on one unit. Per LZ-EH-003, the operational measurements (actions) decisively outweigh the public statement. The MWA is managing a crisis-level operational condition while publicly characterizing it as routine.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: Service continuity prioritized over transparency. MWA's most rational behavior, given their entity profile ('historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs'), is to minimize public and regulatory alarm while hoping Pump 4 returns before a second failure occurs. This is consistent with an operator that fears the political and financial cost of declaring an emergency more than it fears the operational risk of running at capacity. The valve adjustment on Pump 3 is a low-cost, low-visibility intervention — exactly what an organization optimizing for 'no headlines' would do rather than ordering emergency parts or bringing in contract pump capacity.

feedback_loops.loop: Load-Vibration-Failure Cascade: High pump load → accelerated mechanical wear → vibration increase → cavitation risk → pump trip → load redistribution to fewer units → even higher load on remaining units → accelerated wear on those units → cascading failure
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Pump 3 vibration crossing 25 Hz (warning band) or any single pump trip. A Pump 3 trip specifically would be the most likely initiator because it is already showing mechanical stress. If Pump 3 trips, Pumps 1-2 absorb ~148% rated capacity each, immediately breaching Kill Switch #1 and likely accelerating their own vibration signatures toward failure. This is a classic positive feedback loop — each failure makes the next failure more likely and more imminent.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This loop directly threatens the core thesis of 'sufficient mechanical redundancy.' The loop is currently in its early-stage activation: one pump already lost, load redistributed to ceiling, vibration anomaly developing on a second pump. The loop has not yet entered its self-reinforcing phase (no second pump trip), but the distance between current state and self-reinforcing cascade is one discrete event — a single additional pump failure.

feedback_loops.loop: Temperature-Demand-Load Amplification: High ambient temperature → increased water demand (irrigation, cooling) → increased flow requirement → increased pump load → reduced pressure → increased pump stress
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Sustained or increasing temperatures during peak demand hours. The system has zero capacity to absorb demand spikes. Any incremental demand increase cannot be met by increasing pump output (already at ~99%) and would instead manifest as pressure decline. However, per LZ-RC-001, we cannot assert that temperature IS driving elevated demand right now — we have no actual demand data. This loop is plausible but unconfirmed in its demand leg.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: If this loop is active, it means the system is not only mechanically fragile but facing external demand pressure it cannot accommodate. The thesis assumes the system can handle 'periods of peak demand' — 88°F weather during what appears to be a warm season directly tests this claim.

feedback_loops.loop: MWA Communication-Regulatory Inaction Feedback: MWA minimizes operational stress → Regulator sees compliant macro metrics + reassuring public statements → No regulatory intervention triggered → MWA continues operating at limits without external pressure to escalate → Risk accumulates without external circuit breaker
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop shifts to critical when a failure occurs that is visible to the public or regulator — e.g., a boil water notice, a pressure complaint from a fire department, or a visible infrastructure failure. At that point, the communication strategy collapses and the regulator is forced to engage, but reactively rather than preventively. The key insight is that this feedback loop prevents early intervention by design — the regulator only engages after failure, not before.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This loop means the system must self-correct (Pump 4 returns, demand decreases, or MWA proactively intervenes) because the external intervention pathway is effectively disabled. The thesis implicitly assumes some form of oversight backstop — this loop shows the backstop is not operative.

feedback_loops.loop: Intervention-Load Redistribution Trap: Valve adjustment on Pump 3 to reduce vibration → potential reduction in Pump 3 throughput → load shift to Pumps 1-2 → Pumps 1-2 pushed beyond current ~99% → accelerated stress on Pumps 1-2 → potential vibration increase on Pumps 1-2
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: If the valve adjustment successfully reduces Pump 3 vibration but shifts meaningful load to Pumps 1-2, the operators face a trapped position: protecting Pump 3 threatens Pumps 1-2, and vice versa. Any intervention that reduces load on one pump increases load on the others. This is a zero-sum allocation problem with no slack in the system.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is a trapped-position loop. MWA operators cannot improve one pump's condition without worsening the others' conditions. The only resolution is restoring Pump 4 (adding capacity) or reducing demand (which MWA has not signaled doing). The thesis assumption of 'sufficient redundancy during localized equipment maintenance' is directly contradicted by this trapped dynamic.

behavioral_patterns.pattern: Operator minimization under mechanical stress — MWA characterizing a zero-redundancy, 99%-loaded system as 'routine maintenance' mirrors the entity profile of 'historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs.' The behavioral pattern is: minimize → delay → hope for organic resolution → escalate only when forced by visible failure.

Structural information asymmetry enabling risk accumulation — The regulator cannot see pump-level data, and MWA's public communication obscures operational reality. This creates a pattern where risk accumulates in an information vacuum: the only entity with full situational awareness (MWA) has the strongest incentive to downplay it, while the entity with intervention authority (Regulator) lacks the information to act.

Absence of emergency mobilization signals — No evidence of emergency parts procurement, contract pump mobilization, demand management (e.g., conservation notices, irrigation restrictions), or mutual aid requests. If MWA genuinely believed catastrophic failure was imminent, rational behavior would include at least some of these mobilization actions. The absence could indicate: (a) MWA genuinely believes Pump 4 will return quickly, (b) MWA is in denial, or (c) mobilization actions are occurring but not visible in the current data feed. Per LZ-RC-003, absence of evidence of mobilization is not evidence that mobilization is absent.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Three-pump aggregate load near capacity ceiling + Zero redundancy
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Pump 4 went down for planned, short-duration maintenance. MWA calculated that the remaining three pumps could carry full load for the maintenance window. The system is stressed but within MWA's operational planning envelope. This is normal maintenance with temporarily elevated risk that MWA considers acceptable.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: MWA is executing planned maintenance on Pump 4 and expects it to return within a timeframe they consider safe (likely <48 hours, aligning with Kill Switch #1 duration threshold). The maintenance log language ('Load redistributed successfully') suggests this was anticipated. The public statement framing it as 'routine' is consistent with a planned event rather than an emergency.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Pump 4 failure was unplanned and the maintenance framing is face-saving language. The absence of a restoration timeline could indicate uncertainty about the repair scope. If unplanned, MWA may not have a reliable estimate of when Pump 4 returns.
assumptions: MWA has an internal Pump 4 restoration timeline that is short-term
evidence_citations: Maintenance log states 'Load redistributed successfully' — language implies planned redistribution, not emergency response
Public statement says 'Routine maintenance underway' — consistent with planned event
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Pump 4 returns to service within 24-48 hours and aggregate load returns to 70-85% normal range. If Pump 4 does NOT return within 48 hours, the null hypothesis weakens significantly and the alternative (unplanned failure with uncertain timeline) gains weight.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis — planned short maintenance — is the simplest sufficient explanation for the current state. The maintenance log language is forward-planned ('redistributed successfully') rather than reactive. However, this null hypothesis is testable on a short timeline: if Pump 4 doesn't return within 48 hours, the planned-maintenance narrative fails. The critical point is that even if the null holds and this is planned, the risk during the maintenance window is real and extreme — planned or not, any second pump trip produces cascading failure. The null hypothesis explains WHY we're seeing this state but does not reduce the severity of the state itself.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Pump 3 vibration escalating toward cavitation zone
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Pump 3 vibration at 22.4 Hz is elevated but within normal operational variance for a pump running at 98.8% load. Higher load naturally produces higher vibration. The maintenance crew's valve adjustment is a standard precautionary intervention. The vibration will stabilize or decrease after valve adjustment.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Pump 3 may have a pre-existing condition (bearing wear, minor imbalance) that was tolerable at normal load levels but is amplified under extreme loading. The valve adjustment suggests operators know the pump has a vulnerability. This would be consistent with MWA's profile of deferring capital-intensive repairs — running a known-marginal pump rather than taking it offline for rebuild.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The vibration increase is load-induced and any pump would show similar readings at 98.8% load. Pumps 1-2 not showing elevated vibration (12.8-13.0 Hz) would contradict this, but they may simply be newer or more recently serviced.
assumptions: Higher load naturally produces higher vibration within a predictable range for this pump type
evidence_citations: Pump 3 at 22.4 Hz vs Pumps 1-2 at 12.8-13.0 Hz — all at similar load levels (~99%) yet Pump 3 shows nearly double the vibration, suggesting a pump-specific factor not purely load-driven
Maintenance log confirms active intervention specifically on Pump 3 vibration
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If Pump 3 vibration stabilizes or decreases after valve adjustment, null hypothesis is supported. If vibration continues to increase toward 25 Hz despite intervention, the pump-specific pre-existing condition explanation gains weight and the situation escalates. Next reading of Pump 3 vibration is the critical data point.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis (load-induced, will stabilize with valve adjustment) is plausible but partially challenged by an important piece of evidence: Pumps 1-2 are at nearly identical load levels yet show roughly half the vibration of Pump 3. This differential strongly suggests a pump-specific factor beyond pure load correlation. The most parsimonious explanation incorporating this differential is that Pump 3 has a pre-existing mechanical condition being amplified by extreme loading. This is still consistent with a version of the null (no strategic event, just mechanical reality) but it means the valve adjustment may not be sufficient to address the underlying cause. Key uncertainty: we lack vibration trend velocity data to know if 22.4 Hz is stable, slowly rising, or rapidly rising.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: MWA public statement minimizes operational stress
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: MWA's statement is standard operational communication. Utilities routinely issue brief, reassuring statements during maintenance events. The statement is not deceptive — it is simply the standard communications template that does not convey granular operational detail. There is no intent to mislead; the communication just operates at a different level of abstraction than the telemetry data.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: MWA is deliberately minimizing the severity of the situation to avoid triggering regulatory scrutiny, public concern, or political pressure. This is consistent with the entity profile ('historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs'). If MWA acknowledged the true operational state — zero redundancy, 99% loading, vibration anomaly — it would create pressure to declare an emergency, potentially triggering mandatory capital expenditures, regulatory investigation, or public backlash. The incentive to downplay is strong and direct.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The statement was drafted before the operational situation deteriorated to current levels and has not been updated. The gap is one of information latency rather than intentional minimization.
assumptions: MWA communications team has access to current operational data showing the severity of the situation
evidence_citations: MWA public statement: 'Routine maintenance underway. No service interruptions.' — directly contradicted by 99% pump loading and zero redundancy
Thesis entity profile: MWA is 'historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs' — pattern of minimization is documented
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If MWA updates its communication to acknowledge elevated operational stress, conservation requests, or potential service impacts, this would confirm the prior statement was a deliberate minimization. If MWA issues a conservation notice or boil water advisory, the gap between prior statement and reality becomes undeniable.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis — standard template communication — is possible but insufficient to explain the degree of divergence. Describing a zero-redundancy, 99%-loaded system with a vibration anomaly as 'routine maintenance with no service interruptions' goes beyond normal abstraction. A standard template might omit detail, but characterizing the situation as 'routine' is an active mischaracterization of operational risk. Per LZ-EH-003, the operational data (actions) outweigh the statement. The entity profile provides independent corroboration of a pattern of minimization. The primary explanation — deliberate downplay to avoid triggering escalation — requires only one assumption (that MWA comms has access to ops data) and is supported by two independent evidence sources.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Reservoir Alpha at 94.1% — supply-demand decoupling risk
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Reservoir at 94.1% simply reflects normal supply conditions — adequate inflow, normal consumption patterns. High reservoir is an unambiguously positive indicator of supply security. No decoupling is occurring.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: The reservoir level is genuinely positive for supply adequacy. The decoupling hypothesis (Kill Switch #5) requires BOTH high reservoir AND >20% pressure drop from baseline. Without baseline pressure, the decoupling hypothesis cannot be confirmed or denied. Per LZ-EH-005, the data simultaneously supports contradictory interpretations and this contradiction is the finding — it cannot be resolved without baseline pressure data.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The high reservoir could indicate that pumps, despite running at 99% load, are not actually moving water efficiently — the reservoir isn't draining because the distribution system has a downstream bottleneck or the pumps are working hard but producing less effective flow than expected. This would be the decoupling signal.
evidence_citations: System pressure at 66.2 PSI — direct measurement, but baseline unavailable per Layer 2 gap analysis
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If baseline pressure is obtained and shows >20% decline (e.g., baseline was >82.75 PSI), Kill Switch #5 is approached or triggered. If baseline was <82.75 PSI, decoupling hypothesis is rejected. The baseline pressure data is the single most important piece of missing information.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis holds because the simplest explanation — high reservoir means good supply — requires zero assumptions. The decoupling hypothesis requires knowing the baseline pressure, which we don't have. Per LZ-EH-005, I cannot resolve this contradiction without independent tie-breaking evidence. The finding is genuinely unresolved, and that is the correct output per LZ-EPH-001.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Regulatory oversight gap — no real-time telemetry
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The regulatory model was designed for a system with adequate built-in redundancy. Under normal operating conditions, macro-level monitoring (reservoir levels, basic pressure) is sufficient because the system self-corrects through redundancy. The oversight gap is a known design choice, not a failure.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: The regulatory model is functioning as designed — the problem is that the design assumes normal operating conditions with redundancy. The current abnormal state (zero redundancy) falls outside the regulatory model's assumptions. This is not a strategic failure by the regulator; it is a structural vulnerability exposed by specific operating conditions.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: N/A — this is a structural feature, not a strategic action.
evidence_citations: Thesis context: regulator 'lacks real-time telemetry oversight'
Current macro metrics (reservoir 94.1%, pressure 66.2 PSI) appear compliant
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: N/A — structural feature, not a developing situation
strategic_inferences.reasoning: Nothing strategic is happening here. The regulatory structure is what it is. The important analytical insight is not about regulator behavior but about the consequence: there is no external circuit breaker. This amplifies the importance of MWA's own decision-making, which is the key behavioral variable.

hidden_moves.likely_action: MWA has an internal timeline for Pump 4 restoration that it has not disclosed publicly. The maintenance team likely has a repair estimate. If the estimate is short (hours), MWA is rationally choosing to ride out the risk window. If the estimate is long (days+), MWA may be quietly exploring emergency options (contract pumps, mutual aid) without public disclosure to avoid alarm.
hidden_moves.incentive_basis: MWA's primary incentive is continuity of service without triggering public concern, regulatory scrutiny, or mandatory capital expenditure. Disclosing the Pump 4 timeline would invite scrutiny of why they're operating at 99% load. Not disclosing preserves optionality — if Pump 4 returns quickly, no one needs to know how close to failure the system was.
hidden_moves.evidence_hints: The maintenance log confirms Pump 4 is 'offline' for maintenance, implying a known issue being worked. The absence of a public timeline is itself a signal — utilities normally provide ETRs (estimated time of restoration) for planned outages. The omission suggests either the timeline is unfavorable or uncertain.
hidden_moves.materialization_signal: If Pump 4 returns to service with no prior public timeline communication, it confirms MWA was managing the information. If MWA suddenly issues an emergency conservation notice, it confirms the timeline was longer than expected and the situation deteriorated.

hidden_moves.likely_action: MWA maintenance staff may be considering or planning to derate Pump 3 (reduce its load) to protect it from vibration-induced damage, which would require either accepting lower system pressure or issuing conservation/demand reduction measures.
hidden_moves.incentive_basis: If Pump 3's vibration continues to climb, MWA faces a choice: run it hard and risk a second pump failure (catastrophic), or derate it and accept reduced system capacity (degraded service but survivable). A rational operator would choose derating over catastrophic failure. But derating without demand reduction means pressure drops — potentially toward kill switch thresholds.
hidden_moves.evidence_hints: The flow control valve adjustment on Pump 3 is the first step in a derating pathway. If the adjustment reduced Pump 3 throughput, MWA has already begun this process. The next observable signal would be a system-wide pressure decline or a conservation notice.
hidden_moves.materialization_signal: System pressure dropping below 60 PSI without demand change would suggest Pump 3 derating. A conservation notice would confirm demand management is being initiated.

thesis_confirmed.key_evidence: Pump 4 returns to service within 24-48 hours, Pump 3 vibration stabilizes or decreases after valve adjustment, system returns to normal 70-85% loading with full four-pump redundancy. Pressure maintains well above thresholds. All of this must happen without any additional pump issues.

thesis_modified.modification: The thesis claim of 'sufficient mechanical redundancy' must be modified to acknowledge that the system has ZERO redundancy during single-pump maintenance events and that MWA's operational practices create extended periods of critical exposure. The thesis may still hold in the long term (system can deliver water) but the 'even during periods of localized equipment maintenance' qualifier is not currently supported. Modified thesis: 'The RWDN can maintain service under normal conditions but lacks true redundancy during maintenance events, creating windows of critical vulnerability.' Additionally, if baseline pressure data reveals >10% decline, the distribution efficiency claim requires modification.
thesis_modified.key_evidence: Current state: 99% loading, zero redundancy, Pump 3 vibration anomaly, unknown Pump 4 timeline. Even if Pump 4 returns, the fact that a single-pump outage eliminates all redundancy is a permanent structural finding about the system's design margin.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: Pump 3 trips or degrades further (vibration crosses 25 Hz toward 35 Hz), forcing remaining two pumps to ~148% rated capacity — immediately breaching Kill Switch #1. OR baseline pressure data reveals >20% decline from baseline, triggering Kill Switch #5 in conjunction with 94.1% reservoir. OR multi-sector hydrant data reveals pressures below 20 PSI in sectors other than B, triggering Kill Switch #3. The falsification probability is elevated because the system has zero margin — any single additional adverse event triggers a kill switch, and there are multiple plausible adverse events (second pump failure, demand spike, continued vibration escalation).
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Any of: Pump 3 vibration exceeding 25 Hz in next reading. System pressure declining below 55 PSI. Any sector hydrant pressure below 30 PSI. MWA issuing emergency conservation order or boil water advisory. Regional Fire Dispatch staging water tankers.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Currently orderly but with near-zero margin. The system is absorbing maximum stress without failure — all pumps running, pressure maintained, water quality adequate. However, 'orderly' is maintained only because no additional stressor has arrived. The system cannot absorb ANY incremental stress without transitioning to disorderly. This is a brittle equilibrium, not a stable one. The distinction between orderly and disorderly in this system is a single discrete event (one pump trip, one demand spike, one vibration threshold breach).
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: Immediate. The specific catalysts for cascade are: (1) Pump 3 vibration crossing 25 Hz and continuing toward 35 Hz — timeline unknown due to missing velocity data. (2) Any single pump trip — probability elevated by 99% loading and Pump 3 vibration anomaly. (3) A significant demand spike (e.g., fire event, heat wave peak demand, main break increasing flow) — the system has zero capacity to absorb incremental demand. (4) A distribution main break — would increase flow demand and potentially cause localized pressure collapse simultaneously. Each of these catalysts is independently plausible within the current conditions, and they are not mutually exclusive.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: The system is severely pre-loaded on multiple legs simultaneously: (1) Mechanical load: all active pumps at ceiling, zero headroom — PRE-LOADED. (2) Redundancy: zero remaining, single point of failure exposure — PRE-LOADED. (3) Vibration/pump health: Pump 3 trending toward warning band under extreme load — PRE-LOADED. (4) Operator transparency: MWA minimizing situation, reducing likelihood of preemptive external intervention — PRE-LOADED. (5) Regulatory oversight: blind to pump-level stress — PRE-LOADED (structural). Five legs pre-loaded means the system's vulnerability is multiplicative, not additive. The probability of a cascade is the joint probability of ANY ONE catalyst occurring, which is higher than the probability of any specific catalyst.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The system is simultaneously demonstrating resilience (pressure maintained, quality adequate, three pumps carrying full load without failure) and extreme fragility (zero redundancy, one event away from cascade). The thesis of 'robust structural integrity' is technically not yet falsified — the system IS maintaining service — but is doing so at a state where robustness has been entirely consumed. The paradox: every hour the system survives in this state is simultaneously evidence that the thesis holds AND evidence that the thesis is being tested to its absolute limit. If Pump 4 returns without incident, the episode proves the system CAN survive maintenance events — but also proves it does so with zero margin, which contradicts the 'sufficient redundancy' qualifier. The thesis cannot be fully confirmed by surviving this episode; it can only be falsified by failing during it.
